title: HullOFOD8eSolutionsCh13
body:
CHAPTER 13 Wiener Processes and It√¥‚Äôs Lemma

Practice Questions Problem 13.1. What would it mean to assert that the temperature at a certain place follows a Markov process? Do you think that temperatures do, in fact, follow a Markov process?

Imagine that you have to forecast the future temperature from a) the current temperature, b) the history of the temperature in the last week, and c) a knowledge of seasonal averages and seasonal trends. If temperature followed a Markov process, the history of the temperature in the last week would be irrelevant. To answer the second part of the question you might like to consider the following scenario for the first week in May: (i) Monday to Thursday are warm days; today, Friday, is a very cold day. (ii) Monday to Friday are all very cold days. What is your forecast for the weekend? If you are more pessimistic in the case of the second scenario, temperatures do not follow a Markov process.

Problem 13.2. Can a trading rule based on the past history of a stock‚Äôs price ever produce returns that are consistently above average? Discuss.

The first point to make is that any trading strategy can, just because of good luck, produce above average returns. The key question is whether a trading strategy consistently outperforms the market when adjustments are made for risk. It is certainly possible that a trading strategy could do this. However, when enough investors know about the strategy and trade on the basis of the strategy, the profit will disappear. As an illustration of this, consider a phenomenon known as the small firm effect. Portfolios of stocks in small firms appear to have outperformed portfolios of stocks in large firms when appropriate adjustments are made for risk. Research was published about this in the early 1980s and mutual funds were set up to take advantage of the phenomenon. There is some evidence that this has resulted in the phenomenon disappearing.

Problem 13.3. A company‚Äôs cash position, measured in millions of dollars, follows a generalized Wiener process with a drift rate of 0.5 per quarter and a variance rate of 4.0 per quarter. How high does the company‚Äôs initial cash position have to be for the company to have a less than 5% chance of a negative cash position by the end of one year?

Suppose that the company‚Äôs initial cash position is x . The probability distribution of the cash position at the end of one year is œï ( x + 4 √ó 0.5, 4 √ó 4) = œï ( x + 2.0,16) where œï (m, v) is a normal probability distribution with mean m and variance v . The probability of a negative cash position at the end of one year is   Ô£´ x + 2.0 Ô£∂ N Ô£¨‚àí Ô£∑ Ô£≠ 4 Ô£∏

where N ( x) is the cumulative probability that a standardized normal variable (with mean zero and standard deviation 1.0) is less than x . From normal distribution tables

Ô£´ x + 2.0 Ô£∂ N Ô£¨‚àí Ô£∑=0.05 Ô£≠ 4 Ô£∏ when: x + 2.0 ‚àí =‚àí1.6449 i.e., when x = 4.5796 . The initial cash position must therefore be $4.58 million.

Problem 13.4. Variables X 1 and X 2 follow generalized Wiener processes with drift rates ¬µ1 and ¬µ2 and variances œÉ 12 and œÉ 22 . What process does X 1 + X 2 follow if: (a) The changes in X 1 and X 2 in any short interval of time are uncorrelated? (b) There is a correlation œÅ between the changes in X 1 and X 2 in any short interval of time? (a) Suppose that X1 and X2 equal a1 and a2 initially. After a time period of length T , X1 has the probability distribution

œï (a1 + ¬µ1T , œÉ 12T ) and X 2 has a probability distribution

œï (a2 + ¬µ2T , œÉ 22T )

From the property of sums of independent normally distributed variables, X 1 + X 2 has the probability distribution

œï ( a1 + ¬µ1T + a2 + ¬µ2T , œÉ 12T + œÉ 22T )

i.e., œï Ô£∞Ô£Æ a1 + a2 + ( ¬µ1 + ¬µ2 )T , (œÉ 12 + œÉ 22 )T Ô£ªÔ£π This shows that X 1 + X 2 follows a generalized Wiener process with drift rate ¬µ1 + ¬µ2 and variance rate œÉ 12 + œÉ 22 . (b) In this case the change in the value of X 1 + X 2 in a short interval of time ‚àÜt has the probability distribution: œï Ô£∞Ô£Æ( ¬µ1 + ¬µ2 )‚àÜt , (œÉ 12 + œÉ 22 + 2 œÅœÉ 1œÉ 2 )‚àÜt Ô£ªÔ£π If ¬µ1 , ¬µ2 , œÉ 1 , œÉ 2 and œÅ are all constant, arguments similar to those in Section 13.2 show that the change in a longer period of time T is

œï Ô£ÆÔ£∞( ¬µ1 + ¬µ2 )T , (œÉ 12 + œÉ 22 + 2 œÅœÉ 1œÉ 2 )T Ô£πÔ£ª   The variable, X 1 + X 2 , therefore follows a generalized Wiener process with drift rate ¬µ1 + ¬µ2 and variance rate œÉ 12 + œÉ 22 + 2 œÅœÉ 1œÉ 2 .

Problem 13.5. Consider a variable, S , that follows the process

dS ¬µ dt + œÉ dz =

For the first three years, ¬µ = 2 and œÉ = 3 ; for the next three years, ¬µ = 3 and œÉ = 4 . If the initial value of the variable is 5, what is the probability distribution of the value of the variable at the end of year six?

The change in S during the first three years has the probability distribution

œï (2 √ó 3, 9 √ó 3)= œï (6, 27)

The change during the next three years has the probability distribution

œï (3 √ó 3, 16 √ó 3)= œï (9, 48)

The change during the six years is the sum of a variable with probability distribution œï (6, 27) and a variable with probability distribution œï (9, 48) . The probability distribution of the change is therefore œï (6 + 9, 27 + 48)

= œï (15, 75)

Since the initial value of the variable is 5, the probability distribution of the value of the variable at the end of year six is œï (20, 75)

Problem 13.6. Suppose that G is a function of a stock price, S and time. Suppose that œÉ S and œÉ G are the volatilities of S and G . Show that when the expected return of S increases by ŒªœÉ S , the growth rate of G increases by ŒªœÉ G , where Œª is a constant.

From It√¥‚Äôs lemma ‚àÇG œÉ GG = œÉSS ‚àÇS Also the drift of G is ‚àÇG ‚àÇG 1 ‚àÇ 2G 2 2 ¬µS + + œÉ S ‚àÇS ‚àÇt 2 ‚àÇS 2

where ¬µ is the expected return on the stock. When ¬µ increases by ŒªœÉ S , the drift of G  increases by ‚àÇG ŒªœÉ S S ‚àÇS or ŒªœÉ G G

The growth rate of G , therefore, increases by ŒªœÉ G .

Problem 13.7. Stock A and stock B both follow geometric Brownian motion. Changes in any short interval of time are uncorrelated with each other. Does the value of a portfolio consisting of one of stock A and one of stock B follow geometric Brownian motion? Explain your answer.

Define S A , ¬µ A and œÉ A as the stock price, expected return and volatility for stock A. Define S B , ¬µ B and œÉ B as the stock price, expected return and volatility for stock B. Define ‚àÜS A and ‚àÜS B as the change in S A and S B in time ‚àÜt . Since each of the two stocks follows geometric Brownian motion,

‚àÜS= A ¬µ A S A ‚àÜt + œÉ A S AŒµ A ‚àÜt

‚àÜS=B ¬µ B S B ‚àÜt + œÉ B S BŒµ B ‚àÜt where Œµ A and Œµ B are independent random samples from a normal distribution.

‚àÜS B ( ¬µ A S A + ¬µ B S B )‚àÜt + (œÉ A S AŒµ A + œÉ B S BŒµ B ) ‚àÜt ‚àÜS A +=

This cannot be written as

S B ¬µ ( S A + S B )‚àÜt + œÉ ( S A + S B )Œµ ‚àÜt ‚àÜS A + ‚àÜ=

for any constants ¬µ and œÉ . (Neither the drift term nor the stochastic term correspond.) Hence the value of the portfolio does not follow geometric Brownian motion.

Problem 13.8. The process for the stock price in equation (13.8) is ‚àÜS= ¬µ S ‚àÜt + œÉ S Œµ ‚àÜt where ¬µ and œÉ are constant. Explain carefully the difference between this model and each of the following: ‚àÜS = ¬µ ‚àÜt + œÉŒµ ‚àÜt ‚àÜS= ¬µ S ‚àÜt +œÉŒµ ‚àÜt ‚àÜS = ¬µ ‚àÜt +œÉ SŒµ ‚àÜt

Why is the model in equation (13.8) a more appropriate model of stock price behavior than any of these three alternatives?  In: ‚àÜS= ¬µ S ‚àÜt + œÉ S Œµ ‚àÜt the expected increase in the stock price and the variability of the stock price are constant when both are expressed as a proportion (or as a percentage) of the stock price In: ‚àÜS = ¬µ‚àÜt + œÉŒµ ‚àÜt

the expected increase in the stock price and the variability of the stock price are constant in absolute terms. For example, if the expected growth rate is $5 per annum when the stock price is $25, it is also $5 per annum when it is $100. If the standard deviation of weekly stock price movements is $1 when the price is $25, it is also $1 when the price is $100. In: ‚àÜS= ¬µ S ‚àÜt + œÉŒµ ‚àÜt

the expected increase in the stock price is a constant proportion of the stock price while the variability is constant in absolute terms. In: ‚àÜS = ¬µ‚àÜt + œÉ S Œµ ‚àÜt

the expected increase in the stock price is constant in absolute terms while the variability of the proportional stock price change is constant. The model: ‚àÜS= ¬µ S ‚àÜt + œÉ S Œµ ‚àÜt is the most appropriate one since it is most realistic to assume that the expected percentage return and the variability of the percentage return in a short interval are constant.

Problem 13.9. It has been suggested that the short-term interest rate, r , follows the stochastic process

dr =a (b ‚àí r ) dt + rc dz

where a , b , and c are positive constants and dz is a Wiener process. Describe the nature of this process.

The drift rate is a (b ‚àí r ) . Thus, when the interest rate is above b the drift rate is negative and, when the interest rate is below b , the drift rate is positive. The interest rate is therefore continually pulled towards the level b . The rate at which it is pulled toward this level is a . A volatility equal to c is superimposed upon the ‚Äúpull‚Äù or the drift. Suppose a = 0.4 , b = 0.1 and c = 0.15 and the current interest rate is 20% per annum. The interest rate is pulled towards the level of 10% per annum. This can be regarded as a long run average. The current drift is ‚àí4 % per annum so that the expected rate at the end of one year is about 16% per annum. (In fact it is slightly greater than this, because as the interest rate decreases, the ‚Äúpull‚Äù decreases.) Superimposed upon the drift is a volatility of 15% per annum.

Problem 13.10.   Suppose that a stock price, S , follows geometric Brownian motion with expected return ¬µ and volatility œÉ : = dS ¬µ S dt + œÉ S dz

What is the process followed by the variable S n ? Show that S n also follows geometric Brownian motion.

If G ( S , t ) =S n then ‚àÇG / ‚àÇt =0 , ‚àÇG / ‚àÇS = nS n ‚àí1 , and ‚àÇ 2G / ‚àÇS= n(n ‚àí 1) S n ‚àí 2 . Using It√¥‚Äôs lemma: dG = [ ¬µ nG + n(n ‚àí 1)œÉ 2G ] dt + œÉ nG dz This shows that G = S follows geometric Brownian motion where the expected return is n

¬µ n + n(n ‚àí 1)œÉ 2 and the volatility is nœÉ . The stock price S has an expected return of ¬µ and the expected value of ST is S0 e ¬µT . The expected value of STn is [ ¬µ n + 12 n ( n ‚àí1)œÉ 2 ]T S0n e

Problem 13.11. Suppose that x is the yield to maturity with continuous compounding on a zerocoupon bond that pays off $1 at time T . Assume that x follows the process

dx = a ( x0 ‚àí x) dt + sx dz

where a , x0 , and s are positive constants and dz is a Wiener process. What is the process followed by the bond price?

The process followed by B , the bond price, is from It√¥‚Äôs lemma: Ô£Æ Ô£Ø ‚àÇB ‚àÇB 1 ‚àÇ 2 B 2 2 Ô£πÔ£∫ ‚àÇB = dB Ô£Ø a ( x0 ‚àí x) + + s x Ô£∫ dt + sxdz Ô£Ø ‚àÇx ‚àÇt 2 ‚àÇx 2 Ô£∫ ‚àÇx Ô£∞ Ô£ª

Since: B = e ‚àí x (T ‚àí t )

the required partial derivatives are ‚àÇB ‚àí x (T ‚àí t ) = xe = xB ‚àÇt ‚àÇB =‚àí(T ‚àí t )e ‚àí x (T ‚àít ) = ‚àí(T ‚àí t ) B ‚àÇx ‚àÇ2 B =(T ‚àí t ) 2 e ‚àí x (T ‚àít ) = (T ‚àí t ) 2 B ‚àÇx 2 Hence:   Ô£Æ Ô£π dB =Ô£ØÔ£Ø ‚àía ( x0 ‚àí x)(T ‚àí t ) + x + s 2 x 2 (T ‚àí t ) 2 Ô£∫Ô£∫ Bdt ‚àí sx(T ‚àí t ) Bdz Ô£ØÔ£∞ 2 Ô£∫Ô£ª

Problem 13.12 (Excel Spreadsheet) A stock whose price is $30 has an expected return of 9% and a volatility of 20%. In Excel simulate the stock price path over 5 years using monthly time steps and random samples from a normal distribution. Chart the simulated stock price path. By hitting F9 observe how the path changes as the random sample change.

The process is ‚àÜS = 0.09 √ó S √ó ‚àÜt + 0.20 √ó S √ó Œµ √ó ‚àÜt Where ‚àÜt is the length of the time step (=1/12) and Œµ is a random sample from a standard normal distribution.

Further Questions Problem 13.13. Suppose that a stock price has an expected return of 16% per annum and a volatility of 30% per annum. When the stock price at the end of a certain day is $50, calculate the following: (a) The expected stock price at the end of the next day. (b) The standard deviation of the stock price at the end of the next day. (c) The 95% confidence limits for the stock price at the end of the next day.

With the notation in the text

‚àÜS ÔøΩ œï ( ¬µ‚àÜt , œÉ 2 ‚àÜt ) S

In this case S = 50 , ¬µ = 0.16 , œÉ = 0.30 and ‚àÜt = 1 / 365 = 0.00274 . Hence

‚àÜS ÔøΩ œï (0.16 √ó 0.00274, 0.09 √ó 0.00274) = œï (0.00044, 0.000247)

and ‚àÜS ÔøΩ œï (50 √ó 0.00044, 502 √ó 0.000247)

that is, ‚àÜS ÔøΩ œï (0.022, 0.6164)

(a) The expected stock price at the end of the next day is therefore 50.022 (b) The standard deviation of the stock price at the end of the next day is 0.6154 = 0.785 (c) 95% confidence limits for the stock price at the end of the next day are

50.022 ‚àí 1.96 √ó 0.785 and 50.022 + 1.96 √ó 0.785 i.e.,   48.48 and 51.56

Note that some students may consider one trading day rather than one calendar day. Then ‚àÜt = 1 / 252 = 0.00397 . The answer to (a) is then 50.032. The answer to (b) is 0.945. The answers to part (c) are 48.18 and 51.88.

Problem 13.14. A company‚Äôs cash position, measured in millions of dollars, follows a generalized Wiener process with a drift rate of 0.1 per month and a variance rate of 0.16 per month. The initial cash position is 2.0. (a) What are the probability distributions of the cash position after one month, six months, and one year? (b) What are the probabilities of a negative cash position at the end of six months and one year? (c) At what time in the future is the probability of a negative cash position greatest? (a) The probability distributions are:

œï (2.0 + 0.1, 0.16) = œï (2.1, 0.16)

œï (2.0 + 0.6, 0.16 √ó 6)= œï (2.6, 0.96)

œï (2.0 + 1.2, 0.16 √ó12)= œï (3.2,1.96)

(b) The chance of a random sample from œï (2.6, 0.96) being negative is

Ô£´ 2.6 Ô£∂ N Ô£¨‚àí Ô£∑= N (‚àí2.65) Ô£≠ 0.96 Ô£∏

where N ( x) is the cumulative probability that a standardized normal variable [i.e., a variable with probability distribution œï (0,1) ] is less than x . From normal distribution tables N (‚àí2.65) = 0.0040 . Hence the probability of a negative cash position at the end of six months is 0.40%. Similarly the probability of a negative cash position at the end of one year is

Ô£´ 3.2 Ô£∂ N Ô£¨‚àí Ô£∑ = N (‚àí2.30) = 0.0107 Ô£≠ 1.96 Ô£∏

or 1.07%. (c) In general the probability distribution of the cash position at the end of x months is

œï (2.0 + 0.1x, 0.16 x)

The probability of the cash position being negative is maximized when:

2.0 + 0.1x 0.16 x is minimized. Define   2.0 + 0.1x ‚àí1 = = 5 x 2 + 0.25 x 2 y 0.4 x dy = ‚àí2.5 x‚àí 2 + 0.125 x‚àí 2 3 1

dx = x‚àí 2 (‚àí2.5 + 0.125 x)

This is zero when x = 20 and it is easy to verify that d 2 y / dx 2 > 0 for this value of x . It therefore gives a minimum value for y . Hence the probability of a negative cash position is greatest after 20 months.

Problem 13.15. Suppose that x is the yield on a perpetual government bond that pays interest at the rate of $1 per annum. Assume that x is expressed with continuous compounding, that interest is paid continuously on the bond, and that x follows the process

dx = a ( x0 ‚àí x) dt + sx dz

where a , x0 , and s are positive constants and dz is a Wiener process. What is the process followed by the bond price? What is the expected instantaneous return (including interest and capital gains) to the holder of the bond? The process followed by B , the bond price, is from It√¥‚Äôs lemma:

Ô£Æ Ô£Ø ‚àÇB ‚àÇB 1 ‚àÇ 2 B 2 2 Ô£πÔ£∫ ‚àÇB = dB Ô£Ø a ( x0 ‚àí x) + + s x Ô£∫ dt + sxdz Ô£Ø Ô£∞ ‚àÇx ‚àÇt 2 ‚àÇx 2 Ô£∫ Ô£ª ‚àÇx In this case B= x so that: ‚àÇB ‚àÇB 1 ‚àÇ2B 2 = 0; = ‚àí 2; = ‚àÇt ‚àÇx x ‚àÇx 2 x 3 Hence Ô£Æ 1 1 2 2 2Ô£π 1 dB = Ô£ØÔ£∞ ‚àía ( x0 ‚àí x) x 2 + 2 s x x 3 Ô£∫Ô£ª dt ‚àí x 2 sxdz Ô£Æ 1 s2 Ô£π s = Ô£Ø ‚àí a ( x0 ‚àí x ) 2 + Ô£∫ dt ‚àí dz Ô£∞ x xÔ£ª x The expected instantaneous rate at which capital gains are earned from the bond is therefore: 1 s2 ‚àía ( x0 ‚àí x) 2 + x x The expected interest per unit time is 1. The total expected instantaneous return is therefore: 1 s2 1 ‚àí a ( x0 ‚àí x) 2 + x x When expressed as a proportion of the bond price this is: Ô£´ 1 s2 Ô£∂ Ô£´ 1 Ô£∂ Ô£¨ 1 ‚àí a ( x0 ‚àí x ) + Ô£∑ Ô£¨ Ô£∑ Ô£≠ x2 x Ô£∏ Ô£≠ x Ô£∏   a =x ‚àí ( x0 ‚àí x) + s 2 x

Problem 13.16. If S follows the geometric Brownian motion process in equation (13.6), what is the process followed by (a) y = 2S, (b) y=S2 , (c) y=eS, and (d) y=er(T-t)/S. In each case express the coefficients of dt and dz in terms of y rather than S.

(a) In this case ‚àÇy / ‚àÇS = 2 , ‚àÇ 2 y / ‚àÇS 2 =0 , and ‚àÇy / ‚àÇt =0 so that It√¥‚Äôs lemma gives = dy 2 ¬µ S dt + 2œÉ S dz or = dy ¬µ y dt + œÉ y dz (b) In this case ‚àÇy / ‚àÇS = 2 S , ‚àÇ y / ‚àÇS 2 = 2 , and ‚àÇy / ‚àÇt =0 so that It√¥‚Äôs lemma gives

dy =(2 ¬µ S 2 + œÉ 2 S 2 ) dt + 2œÉ S 2 dz or dy = (2 ¬µ + œÉ 2 ) y dt + 2œÉ y dz (c) In this case ‚àÇy / ‚àÇS =e S , ‚àÇ 2 y / ‚àÇS 2 =e S , and ‚àÇy / ‚àÇt =0 so that It√¥‚Äôs lemma gives dy = ( ¬µ Se S + œÉ 2 S 2 e S / 2) dt + œÉ Se S dz or = [ ¬µ y ln y + œÉ 2 y (ln y ) 2 / 2] dt + œÉ y ln y dz dy

(d) In this case ‚àÇy / ‚àÇS = ‚àíe r (T ‚àít ) / S 2 = ‚àí y / S , ‚àÇ 2 y / ‚àÇS= 2e r (T ‚àít ) / S= 2 y / S 2 , and ‚àÇy / ‚àÇt = ‚àíre r (T ‚àít ) / S = ‚àíry so that It√¥‚Äôs lemma gives ( ry ‚àí ¬µ y + œÉ 2 y ) dt ‚àí œÉ y dz dy =‚àí or dy =‚àí(r + ¬µ ‚àí œÉ 2 ) y dt ‚àí œÉ y dz

Problem 13.17. A stock price is currently 50. Its expected return and volatility are 12% and 30%, respectively. What is the probability that the stock price will be greater than 80 in two years? (Hint ST > 80 when ln ST > ln 80 .)

The variable ln ST is normally distributed with mean ln S0 + ( ¬µ ‚àí œÉ 2 / 2)T and standard deviation œÉ T . In this case S0 = 50 , ¬µ = 0.12 , T = 2 , and œÉ = 0.30 so that the mean and standard deviation of ln ST are ln 50 + (0.12 ‚àí 0.32 / 2)2 = 4.062 and 0.3 2 = 0.424 , respectively. Also, ln 80 = 4.382 . The probability that ST > 80 is the same as the probability that ln ST > 4.382 . This is Ô£´ 4.382 ‚àí 4.062 Ô£∂ 1‚àí N Ô£¨ Ô£∑= 1 ‚àí N (0.754) Ô£≠ 0.424 Ô£∏ where N ( x) is the probability that a normally distributed variable with mean zero and standard deviation 1 is less than x . From the tables at the back of the book N (0.754) = 0.775 so that the required probability is 0.225.  Problem 13.18 (See Excel Worksheet) Stock A, whose price is $30, has an expected return of 11% and a volatility of 25%. Stock B, whose price is $40, has an expected return of 15% and a volatility of 30%. The processes driving the returns are correlated with correlation parameter œÅ. In Excel, simulate the two stock price paths over three months using daily time steps and random samples from normal distributions. Chart the results and by hitting F9 observe how the paths change as the random samples change. Consider values of œÅ equal to 0.50, 0.75, and 0.95.

The processes are ‚àÜS A = 0.11 √ó S A √ó ‚àÜt + 0.25 √ó S A √ó Œµ A √ó ‚àÜt ‚àÜS B = 0.15 √ó S B √ó ‚àÜt + 0.30 √ó S B √ó Œµ B √ó ‚àÜt Where ‚àÜt is the length of the time step (=1/252) and the Œµ‚Äôs are correlated samples from standard normal distributions.
